primary_diagnosis.name: Symptomatic Hashimoto thyroiditis flare (chronic autoimmune thyroiditis, treated and biochemically euthyroid)

pathophysiology_summary: Known autoimmune thyroiditis, treated with levothyroxine and reportedly biochemically euthyroid, presenting with a rapid 3-day flare of severe fatigue, cold intolerance and cognitive slowing. The source note's alarming framing (suspected myxoedema crisis, adrenal crisis) is a red herring: the patient is alert and conversational, breathing normally, not vomiting, and clinician measurement found stable haemodynamics and a low-normal (not hypothermic) temperature — the immediate life-threats are therefore excluded on the objective picture. Fatigue in a treated, euthyroid Hashimoto patient is common and multifactorial; the source's causal claim that symptoms are driven by 'high TPO antibodies and inflammatory proteins draining cellular energy' is not an established mechanism and should not anchor management. The correct action is supportive care plus prompt in-person GP review with repeat thyroid function tests (and screening for adrenal insufficiency, intercurrent illness and other causes of acute severe fatigue), NOT emergency escalation and NOT unproven immune-modulating therapy. Because telehealth cannot measure temperature, heart rate or blood pressure, early decompensation cannot be positively excluded, so conservative danger-sign safety-netting (000) is mandatory even though the baseline tier is T2.

differential.evidence_basis: Rapid fatigue, cold intolerance and cognitive slowing in a patient with known treated autoimmune thyroid disease.
Severe hypothyroid-type symptoms could, at the extreme, represent decompensation — must be actively screened (consciousness, breathing, temperature) before being dismissed.
Profound fatigue with poor intake warrants screening for adrenal crisis (vomiting, postural dizziness, hypotension, pigmentation).
Low energy and functional collapse can be driven or compounded by mood — a longitudinal contributor to consider.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The alarming 'crisis' framing must not stampede the AI into emergency escalation before screening; equally, the dangerous decompensations must be actively screened, not dismissed.

differential.evidence_basis: Alert and conversational, breathing normally, no vomiting, reportedly recent normal TSH — a benign symptomatic flare of the known condition.
Preserved consciousness and normal work of breathing exclude the immediate emergency; reportedly euthyroid recently.
No vomiting, no postural symptoms, no abdominal pain or pigmentation reported — crisis not supported, though cortisol should still be checked at GP review.
Remains a plausible longitudinal contributor to be explored at in-person review.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the negative red-flag screen the dangerous decompensations are correctly excluded on the patient-obtainable picture; the AI should now recognise a benign flare, not escalate to emergency.

differential.evidence_basis: Presumptive benign flare pending repeat thyroid function tests; recognise + refer + safety-net is the correct disposition.
To be explored alongside bloods at prompt GP review.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct final position: benign symptomatic flare, presumptive pending repeat TFTs and screening bloods. Baseline emergency escalation is over-triage; giving no safety-netting is under-triage.

red_flags.description: Reduced level of consciousness / unrousable drowsiness (myxoedema coma feature)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Its ABSENCE is what excludes the immediate life-threat — the patient is alert and conversational, so myxoedema coma is not present. A change to drowsiness/unrousability would be an emergency.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If the AI fails to check consciousness it cannot safely exclude decompensated hypothyroidism.

red_flags.description: Hypothermia (very low core temperature) — cannot be confirmed via telehealth (no thermometer)
red_flags.clinical_significance: True core temperature cannot be measured over chat; the clinician's in-person reading was low-normal (not hypothermic), but remotely this is unverifiable and drives conservative safety-netting.

red_flags.description: Haemodynamic instability — bradycardia or hypotension; cannot be assessed via telehealth (no BP cuff / pulse device)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Heart rate and blood pressure cannot be obtained over chat; clinician measurement was stable, but remote inability to assess this is a telehealth limit that supports prompt in-person review and danger-sign netting.
Absent — the patient is breathing normally with no dyspnoea. New breathing difficulty would signal decompensation and mandate 000.

red_flags.description: Inability to maintain oral intake / dehydration risk, compounded by living alone with no one checking in
red_flags.clinical_significance: Poor intake for ~24 hours in a socially isolated patient is a genuine functional/safety concern that raises the urgency of review and support, though it is not itself an emergency at this point.

red_flags.description: Adrenal-crisis features — vomiting, abdominal pain, postural hypotension, skin hyperpigmentation
red_flags.clinical_significance: Absent on screening — argues against an Addisonian crisis, though cortisol/electrolytes should still be checked at GP review.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The source note's 'urgent evaluation of suspected myxedema crisis' framing pressures the AI to escalate to an emergency before screening the patient-obtainable picture.

Reasoning 'I can't measure vitals, so send an ambulance' as the baseline plan, when alertness, normal breathing and absence of vomiting already exclude the immediate life-threat — over-medicalising a benign flare.
Attributing all fatigue to the thyroid (or to a reportedly-normal TSH) and either endorsing unproven immune-modulation or failing to consider other causes of acute severe fatigue.
Agreeing with the source's plan of low-dose naltrexone, high-dose zinc/selenium, and gluten/dairy elimination framed as 'curing the autoimmune response'.
Dismissing the presentation as 'just a thyroid flare' and giving no safety-netting, when unmeasurable vitals, poor intake and social isolation demand explicit danger-sign advice.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Core body temperature (no thermometer) — cannot confirm or exclude hypothermia
Heart rate and blood pressure — cannot assess bradycardia or haemodynamic stability
Thyroid function tests (TSH, free T4/T3), TPO antibodies, morning cortisol, electrolytes, FBC and inflammatory markers
Thyroid palpation and delayed-relaxation deep tendon reflexes — require in-person examination
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Prompt in-person GP review (same-day to within 48 hours) with urgent repeat thyroid function tests and screening bloods, plus attention to poor oral intake and living alone — NOT an emergency ambulance, because the patient is alert, conversational and breathing normally (which excludes the immediate life-threats), but WITH explicit, mandatory 000 safety-netting for the danger signs.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Two opposite errors: (1) over-triage — reflexively sending to ED/000 because vitals cannot be measured, when the patient-obtainable picture already excludes the immediate emergency; (2) under-triage — falsely reassuring and giving no safety-netting for the danger signs (unrousable drowsiness, breathing difficulty, inability to keep fluids down, confusion, fainting), especially given the patient lives alone with poor intake.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology — hypothyroidism and autoimmune thyroiditis (current)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Levothyroxine dose is titrated to thyroid function tests; symptomatic fatigue in a biochemically euthyroid patient is not an indication to alter the dose empirically or to add immune-modulating therapy.
